nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits tropical Indo-West Pacific coral reefs, reef slopes, lagoons, and rubble zones, typically grazing across live rock and coralline-encrusted substrate where it forages on biofilm, microfauna, detritus, and encrusting sponges.

decorPreferences: an established, mature reef system (commonly advised to be at least 6 months cycled) with ample live rock and coralline encrustation for foraging
open sand and rubble zones to wander across
stable water chemistry — sudden swings in salinity, pH, or temperature tend to be poorly tolerated
shaded crevices and overhangs for occasional refuge

diet.notes: Generally regarded as a detritus and microfauna grazer, has been documented feeding on biofilm, micro-organisms, detritus, encrusting sponges, and thin algal films across live rock. In captivity it tends to do best in mature systems where natural grazing supplies most of its nutrition; supplemental feeding with small pieces of shrimp, mussel, clam, sinking pellets, or algae wafers placed near the animal is commonly suggested, though supplemental feeding alone has been reported to be unreliable if natural microfauna is sparse.

goodWith: most LPS, SPS, and soft corals (reef-safe with no documented coral predation)
reef-safe snails, hermit crabs, and other peaceful inverts
other Fromia spp. in sufficiently large, well-grazed systems
avoidWith: harlequin shrimp (Hymenocera picta), which specialize in preying on sea stars
known starfish predators such as triggerfish, large pufferfish, and some wrasses
any system that has been treated with copper-based medications
newly set up or unstable systems with insufficient natural microfauna and biofilm
boisterous tankmates that disturb the substrate enough to bury or stress the star

summary: Striking red-and-cream reticulated Indo-Pacific sea star prized as a reef-safe detritus and microfauna grazer; tends to thrive only in mature, stable reef systems with plentiful live rock.
careNotes: A small to medium reef-safe sea star (family Goniasteridae, order Valvatida — WoRMS-current; older retail sources sometimes still list it under Ophidiasteridae) instantly recognisable by its red-and-white reticulated 'tiled' pattern of raised plates that gives rise to the 'Necklace', 'Tiled', and 'Tile' trade names. Trade overlap with the closely related Fromia milleporella (Red Fromia / Red Tile Star) is common — F. monilis carries the striking patterned reticulation while F. milleporella is solid red with fine dark pores — and both species share similar husbandry. Generally considered reef-safe: there are no consistent reports of it nipping corals or clams. Like other Fromia, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications, abrupt swings in salinity, pH, or oxygen, and should be drip-acclimated slowly (commonly advised over 1–2 hours) and never lifted out of water during handling, since trapped air pockets inside the water-vascular system can lead to tissue necrosis. Care is best treated as intermediate rather than beginner: the species' real risk is starvation in tanks without enough natural microfauna and biofilm, and many failures in the hobby are attributed to placing it in undermatured reef systems. Source spread on key fields was modest and resolved by averaging: maximum size ranges from ~12.7 cm (LiveAquaria) and 8–10 cm (Reeflex / Whitecorals) to 20–30 cm (ReefGuide / Wikipedia, with the 30 cm figure being a rare maximum) — recorded here as 8–15 cm as a practical adult span. Temperature range was averaged across LiveAquaria 22–26 °C and Reeflex 22–29 °C to a conservative 22–26 °C aquarium target. Minimum tank size spread widely across sources (75 L / 20 gal entry-level retail recommendations up to 500 L / 132 gal from European specialist sources); 200 L (~55 gal) is written here as a sensible compromise that biases toward the larger, more conservative end of the range since the species' real constraint is having enough mature live rock and biofilm to graze, not absolute swimming volume.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary. The species' product page at the manifest URL (/product/1093/necklace-sea-star) returned 404; an equivalent LiveAquaria product page for the same species (Tile Sea Star, Fromia monilis, /product/580/) was located via web search and used as the effective primary husbandry source: max size 5 in / 12.7 cm, temperature 22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, omnivore, peaceful, reef-safe, care level moderate, family listed as Ophidiasteridae (note: outdated — current WoRMS placement is Goniasteridae).

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary. Site search did not return a species page for Fromia monilis / Necklace Sea Star — BRS does not appear to stock this species in a catalogued product page. Documented as a failed lookup per the playbook fallback rule; effective secondaries below were used instead.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Fromia monilis
additional.notes: Taxonomy and morphology fallback. Confirms family Goniasteridae, order Valvatida, native to Indian Ocean and Western Pacific from the Andaman Islands to Australia and Japan, maximum diameter ~30 cm (rare). Notes the species 'can live only in an old well-ripened aquarium' and that supplemental feeding is often unsuccessful — drives the cautious 'mature reef only' framing.

additional.name: Reefex (Reeflex) — Fromia monilis
additional.notes: European specialist secondary. Adult size 8–10 cm, temperature 22–29 °C, depth 0–51 m, family Goniasteridae, distribution across Maldives/Indonesia/Philippines/Red Sea, diet detritus + invertebrates + sponges, minimum tank size 500 L. The 500 L recommendation is the upper end of the range used to bias the final tank-size figure toward the conservative side.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org — Fromia monilis
additional.notes: Field-guide secondary used for wild-habitat data: size 7–20 cm, depth 1–37 m, distribution Indo-West Pacific including Micronesia/Indonesia/Philippines/Palau/Australia, family Goniasteridae, order Valvatida. Also documents the additional trade names (Marble, Orange Marble, Red Tile, Necklace, Peppermint Sea Star) consolidated into alsoKnownAs.

additional.name: Whitecorals — Necklace Seastar
additional.notes: European trade-source secondary. Confirms ~10 cm adult size, ~500 L minimum tank size, microfauna-specialist diet, and demanding nature requiring a large well-cycled tank. Generally aligns with Reeflex on the conservative-larger tank-size end.
